did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model trained on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets to classify skin cancer lesions (dermatology task) into seven categories. The model was deployed via TensorFlow.js for real-time classification of dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets for skin cancer classification. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy of 94.28% on the testing set) and describes a proposed methodology and system deployment, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Machine Learning
journal: 5th International Conference on Innovative Practices in Technology and Management (ICIPTM 2026)
brief_summary: This study develops a web-based system for classifying skin cancer lesions using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and TensorFlow.js. The model was trained on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2019 datasets to identify seven categories of skin lesions, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The system is deployed as a React web application that processes images locally in the browser, ensuring data privacy and real-time predictions. The proposed CNN architecture achieved a classification accuracy of 94.28% on the testing set. This result demonstrates the effectiveness of deep learning for automated skin cancer diagnosis and highlights the potential for lightweight, client-side medical image analysis tools.

all_task_types.target: Seven categories of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model that classifies dermoscopic images into seven discrete diagnostic categories using a softmax activation function to return a probability distribution over the classes. The primary task is multi-class classification of skin cancer types.

The paper mentions a segmentation step using thresholding and contour-based extraction to exclude healthy skin and background before classification, but this is presented as a preprocessing step rather than the primary predictive output of the main CNN model.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset for skin cancer classification, also known as the HAM10000: Skin cancer MNIST dataset. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A dataset published by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), also referred to as ISIC 2019: Skin lesion analysis toward melanoma detection. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports model performance metrics (accuracy) but does not perform statistical significance testing against other methods.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset (10,105 images, 7 classes). The paper states the model hit an overall accuracy of 94.28% after training and testing phases. High precision and recall values suggest the model is effective at distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions.

ISIC-2019.notes: The model was also trained on the ISIC-2019 dataset (25,331 images, 8 classes, though classification focused on 7 common classes). The results indicate strong generalization capabilities.

Comparative Baselines.notes: Traditional machine learning methods relying on manual feature extraction reached an accuracy of about 75% to 80%. The proposed CNN model outperformed these baselines by automatically learning relevant features.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_note: ResNet50 mentioned in feature extraction section as enhancing classification accuracy, but the primary model described in Section VII is a custom sequential CNN.

training_details.normalization: Pixel intensity values normalized to range [0,1] by dividing by 255.0

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation using threshold-based region detection and pixel-wise masking to isolate lesion regions and remove background artifacts (hair, air bubbles, irregular skin texture)
Image resizing to fixed spatial resolution of 224 × 224 pixels using resizeNearestNeighbor() interpolation
Pixel intensity normalization to range [0,1] by dividing original pixel values (scale [0,255]) by 255.0
Data augmentation including random rotation (±15°), horizontal and vertical flipping, zoom and scaling, brightness and contrast adjustment, and random cropping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomly rotates images by ±15° to make the model invariant to orientation.
Applied to help the model recognize lesions regardless of symmetry.
Applied to simulate variants in lesion size on the image.
Applied to make the model robust to lighting conditions.
Applied to ensure the model focuses on lesion-relevant areas.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved a classification accuracy of 94.28% on the testing set of the HAM10000 dataset, demonstrating high performance in identifying seven categories of skin lesions.

The model outperformed traditional machine learning methods (such as SVM and Random Forest), which typically achieved accuracies between 75% and 80%, by automatically learning relevant features rather than relying on manual feature extraction.

Deploying the CNN model via TensorFlow.js allows for real-time, client-side inference in a web browser, which enhances data privacy by eliminating the need for backend servers and improves accessibility in resource-limited settings.

The study acknowledges limitations inherent to deep learning models for skin cancer classification, specifically data imbalance, visual similarities between lesion types, and variations in lighting conditions and skin tone, which can affect prediction performance.
The authors suggest that future work should focus on optimizing the framework for mobile and edge devices and validating it with real-world data to further enhance clinical utility.
important_remarks: The paper acknowledges significant limitations inherent to deep learning models for skin cancer classification, specifically data imbalance, visual similarities between lesion types, and variations in lighting conditions and skin tone, which can negatively affect prediction performance.

The study relies on publicly available datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC-2019) and lacks external validation on independent, real-world clinical data, which limits the assessment of generalizability to diverse clinical settings.
The authors explicitly state that future work is needed to validate the framework with real-world data to enhance its clinical utility, indicating that current evidence is preliminary.

While the paper claims high accuracy (94.28% on HAM10000), it does not report statistical significance testing or confidence intervals to support the robustness of these results compared to baselines.

The deployment via TensorFlow.js allows for client-side inference, which enhances data privacy by eliminating backend server requirements, but this approach may face performance constraints on resource-limited devices compared to server-side GPU acceleration.
